---
title: "Which Solver Do I Need?"
subtitle: "A decision guide for choosing the right MLSys·im analytical tool."
---
MLSys·im provides 21 specialized solvers organized across five domains — **Node**, **Data**, **Algorithm**, **Fleet**, and **Analysis** — each resolving a specific physical or logical constraint ("wall") that bounds ML system performance. This page helps you pick the right one.

---

## Start With Your Question

**"How fast will my model run on this GPU?"**
: Use the **SingleNodeSolver** (Roofline). It applies the Iron Law to determine whether your workload is compute-bound or memory-bound and returns latency, throughput, and bottleneck classification.

**"How fast will my LLM generate tokens?"**
: Use the **ServingSolver**. It models the two distinct phases of autoregressive inference: the compute-bound pre-fill (TTFT) and the memory-bound decode (ITL), plus KV-cache memory pressure.

**"What throughput can I get with PagedAttention and continuous batching?"**
: Use the **ContinuousBatchingSolver**. It models iteration-level scheduling and non-contiguous KV cache allocation, calculating throughput gains over static batching.

**"How does inference work on a Cerebras wafer-scale system?"**
: Use the **WeightStreamingSolver**. It models the inverted bottleneck of wafer-scale inference: activations stay on-wafer (SRAM) while weights stream from external MemoryX nodes. The binding constraint shifts from memory bandwidth to injection interconnect bandwidth.

**"Will my inference deployment meet P99 latency SLAs?"**
: Use the **TailLatencySolver**. It models inference servers as M/M/c queues using the Erlang C formula to compute P50 and P99 wait times under variable load.

**"How much MFU do I lose to checkpointing?"**
: Use the **CheckpointSolver**. It calculates checkpoint size (weights + optimizer states), I/O write time, and the resulting MFU penalty based on checkpoint frequency.

**"Is my storage system fast enough for my training data?"**
: Use the **DataSolver**. It analyzes the "Data Wall" — the throughput bottleneck between your storage/interconnect and your compute engine.

**"How big should my model be for this compute budget?"**
: Use the **ScalingSolver**. It applies the Chinchilla scaling laws to find the compute-optimal model size ($P$) and token count ($D$).

**"How long will researchers wait for GPUs on my cluster?"**
: Use the **OrchestrationSolver**. It models the "Wait Wall" in shared clusters using Little's Law and queueing theory.

**"How much accuracy will I lose if I quantize my model?"**
: Use the **CompressionSolver**. It models the trade-off between model efficiency (memory/latency) and task accuracy for quantization and pruning.

**"How does performance scale across multiple GPUs?"**
: Use the **DistributedSolver**. It decomposes workloads using 3D Parallelism (DP, TP, PP) and calculates communication overhead, pipeline bubbles, and scaling efficiency.

**"How much will this cost to run?"**
: Use the **EconomicsSolver**. It calculates Total Cost of Ownership: CapEx (hardware purchase), OpEx (energy + maintenance), and total TCO over a specified duration.

**"What is the carbon footprint?"**
: Use the **SustainabilitySolver**. It computes energy consumption (factoring in PUE), carbon emissions (using regional grid intensity), and water usage across different datacenter locations.

**"How often will my cluster fail during training?"**
: Use the **ReliabilitySolver**. It estimates fleet-wide MTBF, failure probability for a given job duration, and the Young-Daly optimal checkpoint interval.

**"What is the actual MFU my workload will achieve?"**
: Use the **EfficiencySolver**. It models the gap between peak and achieved FLOPS, decomposing MFU by workload type (attention, FFN, conv) and accounting for kernel fusion and FlashAttention.

**"Is my CPU preprocessing keeping up with my accelerator?"**
: Use the **TransformationSolver**. It quantifies the CPU preprocessing bottleneck — JPEG decode, tokenization, augmentation — and whether it starves the accelerator.

**"What network topology should I use?"**
: Use the **TopologySolver**. It calculates bisection bandwidth for fat-tree, dragonfly, torus, and ring topologies to determine effective communication bandwidth.

**"How much does chain-of-thought reasoning cost?"**
: Use the **InferenceScalingSolver**. It models inference-time compute scaling — each reasoning step incurs full autoregressive decode cost.

**"Which hardware parameter should I invest in?"**
: Use the **SensitivitySolver**. It computes numerical partial derivatives to identify the binding constraint — the parameter most worth upgrading.

**"What hardware do I need to meet this latency SLA?"**
: Use the **SynthesisSolver**. It inverts the Roofline model: given a target latency, it derives the minimum bandwidth, FLOPS, and memory required.

**"What is the compute overhead of differential privacy?"**
: Use the **ResponsibleEngineeringSolver**. It quantifies the "ethics tax" — the additional compute, data, and time required for DP-SGD and fairness constraints.

---

## Quick Reference

### Domain 1 — Node (Single-Accelerator Resources)

| Solver | Wall | Key Inputs | Key Outputs | Best For |
|:-------|:-----|:-----------|:------------|:---------|
| **SingleNodeSolver** | 1, 2 | model, hardware, batch_size | latency, throughput, bottleneck | "Is my model memory-bound?" |
| **EfficiencySolver** | 3 | model, hardware, workload_type | MFU, achievable FLOPS, overhead breakdown | "What MFU will my workload achieve?" |
| **ServingSolver** | 2 | model, hardware, seq_len | TTFT, ITL, KV-cache, feasibility | "Can I serve this LLM on this GPU?" |
| **ContinuousBatchingSolver** | 2 | model, hardware, seq_len, max_batch | throughput, fragmentation, speedup | "What throughput with PagedAttention?" |
| **WeightStreamingSolver** | 2, 8 | model, hardware, seq_len, batch_size | throughput, bottleneck, optimal_batch | "Cerebras wafer-scale inference?" |
| **TailLatencySolver** | 13 | arrival_rate, service_latency, replicas | P50, P99, SLO violation probability | "Will I meet P99 latency SLAs?" |

### Domain 2 — Data (Movement & Pipelines)

| Solver | Wall | Key Inputs | Key Outputs | Best For |
|:-------|:-----|:-----------|:------------|:---------|
| **DataSolver** | 4 | workload_data_rate, hardware | utilization, is_stalled, margin | "Is my storage/IO the bottleneck?" |
| **TransformationSolver** | 5 | batch_size, sample_size, cpu_throughput | transform_time, is_bottleneck | "Is CPU preprocessing starving my GPU?" |
| **TopologySolver** | 6 | fabric, topology, num_nodes | effective_bw, bisection_bw, hops | "What topology should I use?" |

### Domain 3 — Algorithm (Scaling & Compression)

| Solver | Wall | Key Inputs | Key Outputs | Best For |
|:-------|:-----|:-----------|:------------|:---------|
| **ScalingSolver** | 7 | compute_budget | optimal_params, optimal_tokens | "What is my optimal model size?" |
| **InferenceScalingSolver** | 8 | model, hardware, reasoning_steps | total_reasoning_time, energy_per_query | "How much does CoT reasoning cost?" |
| **CompressionSolver** | 9 | model, hardware, method | accuracy_delta, compression_ratio | "Is quantization/pruning worth it?" |

### Domain 4 — Fleet (Multi-Node & Society)

| Solver | Wall | Key Inputs | Key Outputs | Best For |
|:-------|:-----|:-----------|:------------|:---------|
| **DistributedSolver** | 10 | model, fleet, tp/pp/dp sizes | scaling efficiency, comm overhead | "How many GPUs do I actually need?" |
| **ReliabilitySolver** | 11 | fleet, job_duration | MTBF, failure probability, checkpoint interval | "Will my training job complete?" |
| **CheckpointSolver** | 4, 11 | model, hardware, optimizer | checkpoint_size, write_time, MFU penalty | "How much MFU do I lose to checkpoints?" |
| **OrchestrationSolver** | 12 | fleet, arrival_rate, avg_duration | avg_wait_time, utilization | "How busy is my cluster?" |
| **EconomicsSolver** | 13 | fleet, duration_days, kwh_price | CapEx, OpEx, total TCO | "What will this cost over 3 years?" |
| **SustainabilitySolver** | 14 | fleet, duration_days, datacenter | energy, carbon (kg CO₂e), water | "Where should I train to minimize carbon?" |
| **ResponsibleEngineeringSolver** | 15 | base_training_time, epsilon | dp_slowdown, effective_training_time | "What is the cost of differential privacy?" |

### Domain 5 — Analysis (Cross-Cutting Diagnostics)

| Solver | Wall | Key Inputs | Key Outputs | Best For |
|:-------|:-----|:-----------|:------------|:---------|
| **SensitivitySolver** | 16 | model, hardware, perturbation_pct | sensitivities, binding_constraint | "Which parameter should I invest in?" |
| **SynthesisSolver** | 17 | model, target_latency | required_bw, required_flops, required_memory | "What hardware do I need for this SLA?" |

---

## Composing Solvers

Real-world questions often require **chaining** multiple solvers. Some examples:

### "Can I serve Llama-70B on 4 H100s within budget?"
1. **ServingSolver** — check if the model fits in memory and estimate TTFT/ITL
2. **EconomicsSolver** — calculate the cost of running that fleet

### "What is the most sustainable way to train GPT-3?"
1. **DistributedSolver** — find the optimal parallelism configuration
2. **SustainabilitySolver** — compare carbon footprint across regions

### "Should I use A100s or H100s for inference?"
1. **SingleNodeSolver** on A100 — get latency and bottleneck
2. **SingleNodeSolver** on H100 — get latency and bottleneck
3. **EconomicsSolver** for each — compare cost per query

### "GPU vs. Cerebras for large-batch inference?"
1. **ContinuousBatchingSolver** on H100 — get PagedAttention throughput
2. **WeightStreamingSolver** on Cerebras CS-3 — get weight streaming throughput
3. **TailLatencySolver** — check P99 SLA compliance for each

### "What is the I/O penalty of checkpointing during training?"
1. **ReliabilitySolver** — determine optimal checkpoint interval from fleet MTBF
2. **CheckpointSolver** — calculate the actual MFU penalty of writing checkpoints at that interval

---

## Textbook Chapter Mapping

Each solver connects to specific chapters in the *Machine Learning Systems* textbook:

| Solver | Domain | Volume 1 Chapters | Volume 2 Chapters |
|:-------|:-------|:-------------------|:-------------------|
| **SingleNodeSolver** | Node | Model Training, Hardware Acceleration, Benchmarking | Performance Engineering |
| **EfficiencySolver** | Node | Hardware Acceleration, Benchmarking | Performance Engineering |
| **ServingSolver** | Node | Model Serving | Inference at Scale |
| **DataSolver** | Data | Data Engineering | — |
| **TransformationSolver** | Data | Data Engineering | — |
| **TopologySolver** | Data | — | Collective Communication |
| **ScalingSolver** | Algorithm | — | Scaling Physics |
| **InferenceScalingSolver** | Algorithm | — | Scaling Physics |
| **CompressionSolver** | Algorithm | Model Compression | — |
| **DistributedSolver** | Fleet | — | Distributed Training, Collective Communication |
| **ReliabilitySolver** | Fleet | — | Fault Tolerance |
| **OrchestrationSolver** | Fleet | — | Compute Infrastructure |
| **EconomicsSolver** | Fleet | — | Compute Infrastructure |
| **SustainabilitySolver** | Fleet | — | Sustainable AI |
| **ResponsibleEngineeringSolver** | Fleet | — | Responsible AI |
| **SensitivitySolver** | Analysis | Benchmarking | Performance Engineering |
| **ContinuousBatchingSolver** | Node | Model Serving | Inference at Scale |
| **WeightStreamingSolver** | Node | — | Hardware Design, Inference at Scale |
| **TailLatencySolver** | Fleet | — | Compute Infrastructure |
| **CheckpointSolver** | Fleet | — | Fault Tolerance |
| **SynthesisSolver** | Analysis | — | Hardware Design |

---

## Code Example

```python
import mlsysim
from mlsysim import SingleNodeSolver, ServingSolver, DataSolver

# Question: Is ResNet-50 memory-bound on A100?
solver = SingleNodeSolver()
profile = solver.solve(
    model=mlsysim.Models.ResNet50,
    hardware=mlsysim.Hardware.Cloud.A100,
    batch_size=1
)
print(f"Bottleneck: {profile.bottleneck}")  # → Memory Bound

# Question: Is my data pipeline a bottleneck for Waymo-scale training?
data_solver = DataSolver()
result = data_solver.solve(
    workload_data_rate=mlsysim.Q_("19 TB/hour"),
    hardware=mlsysim.Hardware.H100
)
print(f"Data Stalled: {result.is_stalled}")
print(f"Utilization:  {result.utilization*100:.1f}%")

# Question: What is the TTFT for Llama-3.1-8B?
serving = ServingSolver()
result = serving.solve(
    model=mlsysim.Models.Language.Llama3_8B,
    hardware=mlsysim.Hardware.Cloud.H100,
    seq_len=2048,
    batch_size=1
)
print(f"TTFT: {result.ttft.to('ms'):~.1f}")
print(f"ITL:  {result.itl.to('ms'):~.2f}")
```

---

## Extending MLSys·im {#extending-mlsysim}

### Why analytical solvers?

MLSys·im (**M**achine **L**earning **Sys**tems **I**nfrastructure **M**odeling) is not an empirical profiler (like PyTorch Profiler) or a cycle-accurate simulator (like gem5). It is an **analytical infrastructure modeling platform** that computes performance bounds from specifications and first-order equations. This is a deliberate design choice:

- **Speed**: Closed-form equations evaluate in microseconds. You can sweep thousands of hardware × model × parallelism configurations in seconds — impossible with empirical profiling.
- **Intuition**: By working from equations rather than opaque traces, students see *exactly* which physical quantity (bandwidth, compute, memory capacity) creates the bottleneck.
- **Accessibility**: No hardware required. A laptop running `pip install mlsysim` gives you the same analysis as a $50,000 GPU cluster.
- **Composability**: Solvers can be chained because they share typed inputs/outputs. The output of one solver feeds naturally into the next.

### Solver architecture

Every solver follows the same pattern:

1. **Takes typed registry objects** — `HardwareNode`, `TransformerWorkload`, `Fleet`, `GridProfile` — as input. These carry physical units (`pint.Quantity`), so dimensional errors are caught at runtime.
2. **Applies first-order equations** from the [Math Foundations](math.qmd) page.
3. **Returns typed results** — every solver returns a typed Pydantic model (e.g., `DistributedResult`, `ServingResult`, `EfficiencyResult`) with `Quantity`-valued fields. Attribute access provides autocomplete, documentation, and type safety.

### Writing a custom solver

You can create your own solver by following the same pattern. Here is a "power efficiency" solver that computes TFLOP/s per watt across the hardware registry:

```python
import mlsysim
from mlsysim.hardware.types import HardwareNode

class PowerEfficiencySolver:
    """Compare hardware on performance-per-watt."""

    def solve(self, hardware: HardwareNode) -> dict:
        if hardware.tdp is None:
            raise ValueError(f"{hardware.name}: no TDP specified")

        flops_per_watt = hardware.compute.peak_flops / hardware.tdp

        return {
            "device": hardware.name,
            "peak_flops": hardware.compute.peak_flops,
            "tdp": hardware.tdp,
            "flops_per_watt": flops_per_watt.to("TFLOPs/s/kW"),
        }

# Use it
solver = PowerEfficiencySolver()

for hw in [mlsysim.Hardware.Cloud.H100, mlsysim.Hardware.Cloud.A100,
           mlsysim.Hardware.Cloud.T4, mlsysim.Hardware.Edge.JetsonOrinNX]:
    r = solver.solve(hw)
    print(f"{r['device']:25s}  {r['flops_per_watt']:>10.1f~}")
```

The key principle: keep your solver's `.solve()` method a pure function of its inputs. Use `pint.Quantity` for all physical calculations so that unit errors are impossible. For production solvers, extend `BaseSolver` and return a typed `SolverResult` subclass — see the [source code](https://github.com/harvard-edge/cs249r_book/tree/main/mlsysim/core/solver.py) for the 21 built-in solvers and [walls.py](https://github.com/harvard-edge/cs249r_book/tree/main/mlsysim/core/walls.py) for the canonical wall taxonomy.

---

*For the equations behind each solver, see [Math Foundations](math.qmd). For API details, see the [Solver API Reference](api/index.qmd).*
